title: Darius “Swami D” Dale’s 2025 NFL Draft Preview

Howdy team 42. Your skipper here at
Darius Dell to present our
2025 NFL draft preview. One of my
favorite days of the year, one of my
favorite things to do. This has been my
uh certainly my longest running hobby.
Um you know, I started scouting the
draft back in 2000. Uh I would uh and
shame on me for doing this, but you
know, I would go because I couldn't
afford it. I would go uh to like
Walgreens or something and put the NFL
draft guide in like a Sunday newspaper
because that was all I could afford. And
and I would literally read cover to
cover every NFL draft guide growing up.
Uh literally every position, you know,
all 230 players or whatever they'd scout
and, you know, just kind of really fell
in love with the draft process. Uh you
know, I'm certainly not unique. This has
obviously blown up into being a gigantic
industry uh thanks to the Godfather
himself, Mel Kyper Jr. uh and and and
all the other wonderful analysts that
contribute to this uh to this wonderful
community. Uh you know, the NFL nailed
it by having two seasons. You know, this
is as uh exciting and me to to me and a
lot of other NFL fans as the actual
season. So, uh without further delay,
let me just hop right into it. So, uh
we'll start with our disclaimer. Uh we
got a lot of clients around the world.
About half of our clients are
international. So, let me remind you
guys when we talk football over here,
we're not talking about soccer. We're
talking about the big boys. And we're
going to spend uh we're going to spend a
lot of time today uh uh talking about
the big boys uh in the trenches. You
know, the game is won and lost uh in the
trenches as evidenced by, you know, what
we saw uh on on early February there
with the buttkicking uh that the Eagles
do out to the Chiefs. You know, there's
two, you know, at the NFL level in my
opinion, there's two ways to win
football games. One, your quarterback is
way better than everybody. Uh and that's
really hard to come by, right? You know,
usually gen generally speaking at any
given time there's probably about four
to six transcending talents at that
position that can really elevate a team
and kind of carry a team to and through
a Super Bowl victory. Uh the other way
to win is having better big people,
right? Football is a game where your 11
guys want to go that way and the other
11 guys are trying to stop you from
going that way. And so obviously that is
best accomplished with big, strong, fast
people who are athletic and and and can
make some things happen in the trenches.
And so uh you know when we do these
draft, you know, previews, you know,
obviously you got the pencil necks out
there running around catching balls,
having a good time, you know, uh and you
got the linebackers and the tight ends
and the fullbacks, you know, kind of,
you know, mixing it up. But really, the
game is won and lost in the trenches, uh
and when with special quarterback play.
And as we said, you know, there's
generally not enough special
quarterbacks that can, you know, carry
their team to and through a Super Bowl.
And so, you got to build your roster, in
my opinion, from inside out. You want to
have better O lineman and D lineman than
the other team. And if you do, chances
are you're probably going to win the
game. So, uh, let's get in let's get
into it. Uh, we'll start with a, uh,
kind of a review of, uh, of what we, uh,
said in the previous years. So, we'll
start with 2022. Uh we were high on
higher on Jameson Williams, Germaine
Johnson and Malik Willis relative to
consensus. Uh Jameson Williams hit,
Germaine Johnson hit. Uh both about
played in my opinion, their draft
position. Uh dead wrong on Malik Willis,
although I'm not giving up hope yet. Uh
but yeah, pretty clear he's not going to
be a star quarterback. So definitely
dead wrong on that. Uh and then we
nailed the stock down. Uh Trayvon Walker
has turned into an average at best
player, taking first overall above Aiden
Hutcherson. Come on, guys. What were you
guys thinking? That's why you're the
Jags. Still drafting in the top 10. Uh
Trevor Penning, uh I want to say I think
he went to the Saints, but just, you
know, really all the stuff that I talked
about with him exposing his chest, it's
really shown up in the NFL and he's
turned out turned out to be a great
player. Uh Inky Auano's uh gotten
better. He's much better player than he
had been, but certainly uh has uh
underplayed his draft position as a six
overall pick. He's not even the best
tackle, you know, in that in that top
10. uh uh you know, I would argue

for the Seahawks uh getting into 2023 uh
nailed Nolan Smith and Will McDonald.
Again, when you see defensive end, I'm
going let you guys in on a little trade
secret here when it comes to scouting
defensive ends. If you see a guy with a
two-way go, he should go in the top 10.
I think Nolan Smith was number 30. Uh
Will McDonald drafted at 15. Come on,
guys. What are you doing? What are you
doing? Will McDonald, there are maybe
eight to 10 people on earth that can
bend the edge at like four and a half
yards and Will McDonald's one of them.
That guy doesn't get out of the top 10.
And they made a mistake and obviously
he's outplayed that. He's going to be a
double- digit sack guy for years to
come. Uh as is Nolan Smith, you know,
godly he I mean in the run game, even

hits people pulling guards and takes on

Drake May, uh, if I could go back and do

you know, back at the top of tier three.
Kind of where he was in the consensus
board. Uh, that was essentially um, uh,
where he was in the consensus board. I
just didn't see it last year. His
footwork was sloppy and led to a lot of
missed error throws. But again, you're
seeing more and more of these guys come
into the league now where they're
cleaning up their mechanics and they're
cleaning up their accuracy in ways that
you didn't historically see. Uh, and so
I think, you know, old heads like myself
who've been scouting in the draft for
what is 25 years now, you know, uh, I
think we have to adjust and start to
give these guys a little bit more grace
with respect to their mechanics. Uh,
because if they have all the other
stuff, then, you know, you can assume
that they're going to get a little bit
better mechanically. Um, but obviously
you prefer to see it. You know, that's
that's let you enter another another
trade secret as relates to scouting NFL
draft. When it comes to quarterback
play, you want to see it and you want to
see a lot of it, right? You know, was it
Bill Parcell said, "I need a minimum of
30 starts or 36 starts or something like
that from his quarterbacks." Uh, you
know, I can go on a diet tribe for this,
but there's so many things when it comes
to scouting quarterbacks that are um
that makes it really hard going from
college to NFL. One, the hashes are like
half as wide. And so, what what
ultimately happens is you have way more
space when you're on one hash or another
in college. And so, it creates way, you
know, way less traffic. It's a lot
easier to see. Routes develop easier.
You have more spacing with the route
concepts. Uh, you also just have less
fast people running around trying to
cover people and and and and closing
holes in zones. All that is untrue in
the NFL. In the NFL, everybody's
compressed in the middle of the field.
Everybody's really tall, really fast,
really long, and holes compress
instantaneously. Z, I mean, it's just
instantaneously. Guys open, guys close.
Guys open, guys close. And even in man
coverage, you know, obviously college
DBs are not, you know, they're going to
be doing something else on Sunday when
they're done done graduating college,
whereas NFL DVs, you know, capable of
running around these freaks, uh, and and
and and maintaining leverage and
coverage and whatnot. So, uh, that stuff
is, you know, super important. So, it's
really what I'm trying to say is the
reason NFL is so bad at drafting
quarterbacks is because it's almost
impossible to to to, uh, to see the
quarterbacks do the things in college
that you need them to do in the NFL. You
just don't see it. you if you'd saw it,
that would just be akin to a college
coach making their his own team's job
harder to score points. It's easier to
score points during these bubble screens
and zone reads and and all these kind of
play action shot plays where the the
linebacker's out of position, all this
kind of stuff in in college, whereas you
just don't see that stuff uh play after
play after play after play after play in
the NFL. Rarely do you see that stuff.
So, what you need in the NFL is a guy to
be able to drop back immediately, find
the open receiver, and get rid of the
ball uh as quickly as possible. You just
don't see a lot of guys hitting the top
of their drop and getting rid of the
ball. To me, that's the number one thing
I watch for when I'm scouting
quarterbacks is when that foot hits the
top of the drop, is the ball coming out
of his hand? And if the ball does not
come out of the hand at the top of the
drop, it tells me that it doesn't
process information as fast as it needs
to to process information at the NFL
level. you know, who's the best the
person I saw the best do that and coming
out of college is Joe Joey B. Uh
obviously lighting up down there uh
having an MVP caliber season uh uh in um
in in Cincinnati. And so uh one final
thing I'll say on these um tiers is uh
you know we kind of grade you know I
think NFL drafts the the smart GMs not
every GM but the ones you that
consistently put their teams kind of in
the top eight final four and into the
Super Bowl uh uh uh discussion in my
opinion they draft horizontally
according to tiers. You know, all the
stuff that you see online with these
consensus rankings and everything, it's
all ranking, ranking, ranking. This
guy's 35th and this guy's 37th and, you
know, this guy's 15th and that guy's
21st. Who cares? The rankings mean
nothing. The rankings mean absolutely
nothing. What matters are the tiers. Are
what tier are they in? And that's how
you should draft. Never draft. If
there's a guy on the board and you have
a position of need, here's how the best
GMs draft. Here's how Roseman drafts in
my opinion. you go the best player
available according to the tier. And
obviously if you have a need and there's
two guys with similar grades in the same
tier, then take your need. But if you
have a need below, you have a player of
need and a tier below, you have to
ignore that that need and just take the
best available player. That's why he
winds up with a Dline that includes
Jaylen Carter and Nolan Smith and and uh
and what's the other big boy um from
Georgia, too. Uh uh you know, everyone,
you know what I'm talking about. But
again, this is why you wind up with
great offensive and defensive line play
because they understand the positional
value of offensive and defensive
linemen. And that's why you see the
Eagles consistently draft offensive and
defensive linemen in the first round, in
the second round. They usually don't
wait. They wait till the third, fourth,
fifth round before they start attacking
other positions, generally speaking. Uh,
and um, you know, Hi Roseman is just
constantly running circles around the
NFL, and I'm very jealous as a Seahawks
fan. So, let me shut up and get to our
2025 uh uh uh draft preview. So, uh the
first thing you should notice uh when
you go from 2024 to 2025 is the lack of
words up here. Um you know, in tiers one
and two. Last year, we had two
quarterbacks in tier one. Uh uh and so
let me actually explain tiers before I
go back to that. Tier one is uh what we
consider to be an immediate to average
to above average starter as a rookie
with all pro upside in three years. So
expect them to wind up on an allp pro
team. It's within three years. Uh tier
two is immediate average to below
average starter with allp pro upside in
three years. This is someone who very
clearly has the tools and the freakiness
to be one of the best players at his
position in the NFL, but probably needs
a little bit of technical refinement or
coming off a major injury or some, you
know, some there's some red flag that
says he's not going to hit the ground
running day one, but you can very
clearly see a path to them being a best
player, one of the best players in the
position in the NFL. So that's tier two.
Tier three is an immediate average to
above average starter with Pro Bowl
upside in three years. So Pro Bowl
you're, you know, you're talk, so allp
pro, you're basically saying you're top,
you know, two to four, two to five at
your position in the National Football
League. Pro Bowl, you're probably top
five to 15, five to 20 uh uh at your
position. Uh in terms of Pro Bowl
potential in the National Football
League was obviously very good player,
you know, that's a excellent player, one
of the best players on most teams. So,
um, you know, that's kind of that's
that's our tier three. You know,
somebody who can hit the ground running
with probo upside. Uh, tier four is
somebody who's probably not going to hit
the ground running with the same degree
of, you know, same, you know, output and
and and and um and and and production.
Uh, but they also still have Pro Bowl
upside. You could envision this person
being among the best players at their
position uh within three years in the
National Football League. Again, you
know, kind of all pros top five, Pro
Bowl's kind of five to kind of 15. If
you think about that in terms of um in
terms of ranking players across the
league, uh and then tier five would be
immediate average to above average
starter with solid starter upside. So
this the good teams in the NFL have a
lot of tier five and tier six type
players on it, right? You're only going
to have so many I unless you're the
Howie Roseman and the Eagles, you're
only going to have so many Pro Bowl and
all procaliber players in your team. For
one, you only get seven draft picks and
really in most drafts only three of them
are valuable the first three rounds
generally speaking. Um, if you get a if
you hit on a fourth, fifth or sixth or
seventh round pick, it's r it's more
luck than anything. By the way, the
whole draft is luck. Uh, there was a 20
2004 white paper uh published by um dude
who published that white paper. It was
uh one of like the Richard Thaylor I
think wrote the paper, one of the top
behavioral economics professors in the
world. Uh uh no, I think he may have won
a Nobel Prize not for the paper but for
something else behavioral economics
related. And what he basically found out
was that uh the probability that an NFL
team selects a player better than the
player they selected in the draft uh
sorry that the probability that an NFL
team selects a player that is better
than the pick immediately after them is
51%. When they analyze the entire NFL
draft and it's only like 60% in the
first round. Like it it's obviously
scales. It's easier to see the first
round guys and the second round guys and
so on and so forth. It's kind of like
the Ricker scale, but it tells you that
this thing is a crapshoot. And part of
the reason it's a crapshoot is not
because NFL GMs and scouts don't know
what they're doing. They obviously know
what they're doing. It's just that you
don't get to see a lot of NFL football
being played on Saturdays especially
anymore. You know, I don't know what's
going on with these Gen Z coaches, but
but like no offense, it's a lot of
horizontal. It's a lot of patty cake up
front. It's a lot of lot of, you know,
just a lot of catching up on the
offensive line. It's a lot of standing
up and reading as a defensive line.
there's not a lot of real violence and
that's what happens in the trenches.
Obviously, I talked about how different
playing college quarterback is in the
NFL quarterback. You know, there's
nobody around you in college. There's
all this open space in college and it's
the exact opposite in both cases uh
playing quarterback in the NFL. So, you
just don't see a lot of NFL type
football uh until you get to Sunday. So,
it's just hard to project. And so, a lot
of times what we rely on is measurables
and and and and you know, all-star game
setting type competition, one-on-one
drills down bowl. We'll talk about that
shortly. That's the kind of stuff you
kind of gives you a little bit more
clues and more pieces to the puzzle. Uh
so, um you know, don't give your give
your GM and your scouting department if
you're an NFL fan a little bit more
grace because this stuff is hard um in
terms you can kind of see it with
people. Uh especially when you're
watching Big 10 and SEC football, but
you know, when you start watching, you
know, no offense, the Big 12 or, you
know, or any other league ACC, that's
kind of tier two there. And then beyond
that, it's like it's a total crapshoot.
Most of the guys you're watching,
including the prospects you're
evaluating, are playing against people
who certainly won't be playing in
national football and or if they are,
they're not going to be very good
players. And so, what you want to see is
big on big, good on good one-on- ons.
Uh, and you don't see a lot of that in
college. So, it does make this process
harder. Um, but, you know, that's that's
why these guys get paid millions of
dollars. So, uh, without further delay,

my opinion are, you know, outside of
Dline. These are the four most important
positions. These are the first round

cornerbacks and receivers and linemen or

in the first round unless they're
generational talents. Unless you know,

this person being in the Pro Bowl within

supply and demand imbalance. There are
less good O linemen and less great
Dlinemen in rounds two, three, four,
five. You pretty much run out of them by

my opinion, what should happen and Howie
Roseman certainly exploits this as the
mastermind behind the Eagles, he
understands the positional value in the
draft probably better than anybody in
NFL history. In fact, I think he is the
best general manager in NFL history. He

free agency era is unprecedented and may
never be repeated again in terms of his
ability to build rosters and and
consistently um outmaneuver uh the rest

he's a quarterback. It's a supply and
demand imbalance. So, you, you know, the
price is the price. Uh, and that's
another thing I see NFL GMs don't
understand price. John Snder, if you
listen to me talk right now, our
Seahawks GM, you don't understand how
economics works. You think your price is
the price. And it's not true. The NFL,
the market for talent determines the
price. It's not what you think the price
is for offensive linemen. It's what the
market determines the price is for
offensive linemen. So, if anybody knows
John Snder, can send this to him, please
send this to him so he can stop ignoring
the offensive line in the top, you know,
few rounds. Uh, and then there's a lot.
And so, like I said in this um this uh
this offensive line class, it's a good
class. I like a lot of the guys. You
know, you got a lot of guys in tier
three and tier four, a lot of guys in
tier six here. Uh more guys in overall
in this kind of top six tiers, which is,
you know, the bulk of the good players
on any roster will be you within top
tier one, two, three, four, five or six.
you know, this is these are the good
players on your team. Folks in tier
seven, eight, nine, 10, you know, those
are the below average players on your
team that you're looking to the draft
and free agency to replace, right? You
got to put 11 guys on the field on
offense. You got to put 11 guys on the
field on defense, but not all 11 are
guys you want sticking around for
multiple years. Uh, and so that's kind
of those the guys you're looking into
the draft to replace, the guys you're
looking for agency to replace, they're
down in tiers seven through 10. And so,
uh, you know, so like I said, there's a
lot of solid starters and Pro Bowl
upside type guys. Now again, just
because there's a lot of guys that have
Pro Bowl upside doesn't mean they're
going to make the Pro Bowl. You still
have to make the Pro Bowl, but what I'm
essentially saying is they have the
potential to make the Pro Bowl. It's not
a hard no that they're not going to. And
so that's great. You know, if you need
all line help, this is a great draft for
that. If you want a generational left
tackle, terrible draft for that. There
are none. If you want a generational
guard, uh I think there's one. We'll
talk about him in a second. I think
there's one. Uh but by by definition,
not a lot of generational talent in this
draft, period. Uh especially at the
positions that you should be drafting in
the first round with your quarterback,
offensive line, and defensive line
period. Um so let's get into it. Uh how
we grade these guys. Um you know, so
obviously played offensive line at Yale,
was highly decorated player uh in
college. Was a UDFA uh um uh uh prospect
for the Seahawks. Got a call after the
draft uh to get invited to mini camp. Uh
I had already lost weight and kind of
gave up my playing career. I wanted to
get started on on Wall Street. Uh uh I
knew I wasn't good enough to to last. So
I didn't want to waste my time and
wanted to put my Yale degree to work.
But uh so I think I know what I'm
talking about here when it comes to
evaluating offensive line play. That was
again like I said, four year starter at
Yale, highly decorated, really really
good player, NFL prospect, uh myself. Um
when I evaluate offensive line, and
again there's a lot of people here. Most
years you're not going to see this many
guys with, you know, third round type
first to third round type grades. I
mean, this this 21 guys here, you know,
most years you're talking about 10 12
guys. So, this is a it's a solid. You're
going to get if you need O line help,
and raise your hand if you need line
help. I think everybody out there,
unless you're an Eagles fan, probably
raised their hand. Unless you're Eagles
or Bucks fan or a Lions fan, you should
have raised your hand because you need O
line help. Everyone needs a line help.
You see all these freaks coming after
these quarterbacks in the NFL. You know,
there's two ways to win NFL. Like I
said, you have a quarterback that's a
transcendent talent and there's only any
given time four to six of those people.
And uh the rest the only other way to
win is have your big guys be better than
the other team's big guys. And so uh you
know this is going to be a good draft
for improving the quality of your own
line, but it's not going to be a great
draft like I said for you know acquiring
elite talent. You know like the you know
the best players of the world like the
Jamar Chases or the or the um or the Pat
Mahomes of the world. You know you're
just not going to that's that's not this
draft. But it's a great draft in general
because there's a lot of really really
talented, you know, back half of the
first round all the way down through the
kind of top half, third, you know, of
the third round type guys. That's a lot
of people. Um, and it's going to make a
lot of football teams better. So, uh,
getting into how I think about grading
the offensive line, uh, I would say
there's probably five areas of that that
I'm evaluating. Whenever I'm evaluating
offensive linemen, uh, I'm looking at
footwork, u, you know, I'm looking at
hand placement, I'm looking at pass
blocking, I'm looking at run blocking,
and I'm looking at finish. Uh, and then,
uh, you know, we average those grades
out, and then we adjust the grade for
position. Uh, no offense to my friends
or guards, and no offense to my interior
line friends, but we all know playing
tackle is much harder than it is playing
guard. The fur the closer you get to the
ball as an alignment, the easier it is
to play. The centers always have help,
especially when there's a shade or or,
you know, somebody on on them, a zero or
a shade on them. They're going to have
help. The guard help in most most most
most plays, unless there's somebody
walked up and mugged in the A gap or
something like that. Uh guards, it's
harder to play guard than it is to play
center. You don't often have help. You
know, I love to say you gota, you know,
you got to reach a three technique.
That's one of the hardest things to do.
Uh in football, u that, you know, but
but generally speaking, when you're
talking about who has the hardest job in
the National Football League outside of
playing quarterback, I would say it's
got to be the tackles. You got to block
the Miles G. You have to block the Miles
Garretts and the and the and the Michael
Parsons and the and the TJ Watts and and
the and the Nick Bosas of the world. I
mean, good lord, these guys are
enormous. They're incredibly fast.
They're Olympic sprinter fast. They're
enormous. They're incredibly strong. And
they generate so much force. I don't
think the average person who's certainly
not who hasn't played football
understands how much force is generated
when these people hit each other. I
mean, someone who's 275 pounds running a
4540 or 4440 running into somebody
who's, you know, 325 pounds. I mean, it
is it's a car crash. Every single play
when you're talking about dropping back,
deep drop pad, drop back pass, third and
long type pass plays as a tackle. Well,
you got to absorb a lot of force. You
got to move really quickly because these
guys are really fast. They're really
bendy. Uh, and they're really strong,
you know. So, it's a hard it's a very
hard position to play. And there's not
many guys on earth who can play it, let
alone play it well. And so that's why,
in my opinion, it's such a premium
position in the draft. There's just not
that many people that can do it, let
alone do it at a good uh level. So, uh
getting back into uh uh the grades, you
know, when I'm evaluating footwork, you
know, we kind of you have to have like a
avatar of what you're looking for as a
scout or that's another trade secret if
you're a young scout. Don't just go out
there and say, I think this guy's good
or bad or I like him or don't like him.
start evaluating very specific traits
that you need to be successful at the
position and have a real clear
distinction in mind of what success
looks like. You know, when it comes to
footwork, uh, you know, when it comes to
footwork, we, you know, we're talking
about Trent Williams here. I think he's
got the best feet of anybody I've seen.

I've seen. So, let's just take a little

feet here. You know, what you want to
see as a tackle is a guy whose feet
never leave the ground in pass
protection. What you don't want to see
is a guy who's plotting and feet are
going up and down and, you know, he's
he's belaboring to get to his the top of
his drop and to get to his points. You
want to see guys who can just slide
effortlessly like they're, you know,

there's uh there's games within the

every play at this level. you know, the
the the hands of the D linemen are so
good and so quick and and as a function
of that, not only do you it puts the
onus on the offensive line to get their
hands right where they need to be, but
they also have to reset their hands and
keep their body clean and and and and
get the defensive lineman's hands off of

Jonathan Ogden, can't get around him,
can't get through him, and if you piss
him off enough, he's going to bury you.
You know, that's what you want. Run
blocking. Walter Jones, proper angles,
getting your hat where it needs to be so
you can threaten the leverage at the DN.
That's why he was able to drive Mike
Rucker 15 yards downfield. He reached
him and Mike Rucker said, "Oh my god, I
got reached." Coach said, "Don't get
reached." So Mike Rucker's focusing on
not get getting unreached, going that
way. And Walter Jones with his 325 pound
butt is going this way. So guess what's
going to happen? Mike Rucker's going to
wind up on the ground 15 yards later. So
when you talk about run blocking, it's
not just who's bigger and stronger.
You're never going to just bigger and
stronger person at the National Football
League. It's can you get the right
angles to create movement? And when you
hit those angles, are you strong enough
to actually follow through and run your
feet and run these people and dump
people on piles and that at the end of
the day finish the dumping the people on
the pile? That's the fun part. You know,
if you're an old lineman, you don't
really get to do much, right? You you
don't have any stats. It's almost like
you didn't even participate in the game
if you watching the newspaper. And quite
frankly, you want to stay out of the
newspaper as a whole alignment. You
don't want to be in the newspaper as an
alignment. At the end of the season, you
want to be in the newspaper, but during
the season, you don't want to be in the
newspaper because that means you're
doing your job poorly. Uh the the the
football game is enjoy best enjoyed
watching the the the skinny people run
around because it's fun to watch. Uh as
an O lineman, what's fun to watch and
what's fun to play is the finish. Are
you dumping guys? Are you running people
through piles? Are you creating traffic
jams and burying people? And you know
why that matters? Because the big plays
happen when the O linemen finish. When
you let guys slip off blocks, you you
let guys get to the running back, you
let guys get to the the guys downfield
chasing for for for um for for fighting
for extra yardage, you know, when you
let that stuff happens, it it it it, you
know, you don't have as many big plays
as a team. You know, you probably can
sneak one big play a game just from
somebody finishing a block out, you
know, uh out in space up front. Um so
that's that stuff's super important.
There's another reason why you want to
finish as an old lineman. It's harder to
play defensive line. You're doing the
same thing. But you know what defensive
linemen have to do that old linemen
don't? Tackle people. At some point you
have to disengage from the giant man
trying to move you out of the way and
also bring down somebody who's really
fast and probably also really strong.
And so that drains energy. That's why
you see Dlineman rotate and O lineman
don't rotate. Right? Both people are
getting tired over the course of the
game. But if they played the same number
of snaps, the D lineman will get much
more tired. And you can accelerate the
them getting tired by finishing them,
forcing them to get off the ground
constantly, constantly burying them,
pushing them on the ground, dumping them
over piles. That stuff is annoying as a
D lineman. That stuff makes you super
tired and it just speeds up the process
by which they get fatigued so you can
start leaning on them in the fourth
quarter and creating that push in the
run game so you can close out football
games like the best teams do. And so
that's why finish is super important as
well. So, uh, let's get into it here,
and I'll be quick with the actual
players because I want to get back to

certainly, yeah, he's one of the best
pass blockers in the draft. Just fluid,
smooth mover. It's just fluid and
smooth. One thing I he annoys me though,
he opens his hips a little bit too early
in his pass set. I think that's
something he's gonna have to fix in the
NFL because if you start doing that,
what's going to happen is you're going
to get a lot of cross face, a lot of
guys beating you on the inside, and more
importantly, you're going to get a lot
of bull rush. When you open your hips
early, if you don't keep your hips
square to the line of scrimmage as long
as possible, you're going to get bull
rush. Second you turn your body like
this and that that car crash is coming
right at you, where's your butt pointed?
Your butt's pointed at the quarterback.
You want your butt pointed that way
towards the end zone when the car crash
happens so that you go that way and not
into the quarterback. And so he's got to
clean that up, but he's otherwise a
really good football player. Um, you
know, solid in the run game, but not not
